message.content:
{
  "materials": {
    "ppt": {
      "templateId": "visual-focus",
      "templateName": "Visual Focus Classroom Template",
      "themeId": "cbse-academic-blue",
      "title": "Introduction to Photosynthesis - Grade 7 Science",
      "presentationTitle": "How Plants Make Food: The Process of Photosynthesis",
      "presentationGoal": "To enable students to understand the process, requirements, and importance of photosynthesis through visual demonstration and guided practice.",
      "audience": "Grade 7 Students (Age 12-13)",
      "theme": "cbse-academic-blue",
      "themeTokens": {
        "fonts": {
          "heading": "Montserrat Bold",
          "body": "Open Sans Regular"
        },
        "colors": {
          "primary": "#0D47A1",
          "secondary": "#2E7D32",
          "accent": "#FFC107",
          "background": "#F5F9FC",
          "surface": "#FFFFFF",
          "text": "#263238",
          "mutedText": "#546E7A"
        },
        "visualStyle": {
          "topBarStyle": "solid-primary-with-icon",
          "cardStyle": "rounded-shadow-elevation-1",
          "visualFrameStyle": "circular-green-accent"
        }
      },
      "assetSearchPlan": {
        "preferredSources": [
          "Wikimedia Commons",
          "Unsplash Education",
          "Khan Academy Visuals"
        ],
        "safeSearch": true,
        "licensingNotes": [
          "Ensure all images are CC0 or Creative Commons Attribution for educational use.",
          "Avoid copyrighted textbook diagrams; recreate using SVG where possible."
        ],
        "fallbackStrategy": "Use generated SVG diagrams if external image search fails to find suitable high-contrast scientific illustrations."
      },
      "licenseChecklist": [
        "Verify all student-facing images are free for educational redistribution.",
        "Ensure no proprietary textbook figures are used without permission.",
        "Confirm attribution text is accurate for any third-party assets."
      ],
      "presentationWarnings": [
        "Do not read directly from the slide bullets; use speaker notes for detailed explanations.",
        "Allow 2 minutes of silence during Slide 7 visual analysis to let students observe details.",
        "Ensure classroom lighting allows visibility of the diagram on Slide 7."
      ],
      "coverageSummary": {
        "learningOutcomesCovered": [
          "Define photosynthesis and its importance for life.",
          "Identify the raw materials (inputs) required for photosynthesis.",
          "Explain the role of chlorophyll in capturing light energy.",
          "Describe the products released during the process."
        ],
        "topicsCovered": [
          "Definition of Photosynthesis",
          "Inputs: Sunlight, Water, Carbon Dioxide",
          "Outputs: Glucose, Oxygen",
          "Role of Chlorophyll and Leaves"
        ],
        "taughtConceptsCovered": [
          "Light energy conversion to chemical energy",
          "Gas exchange through stomata (implied)",
          "Food chain foundation concept"
        ],
        "omittedContent": [
          "Detailed Calvin Cycle steps (reserved for Session 2)",
          "Chemical equation balancing details (Session 3)"
        ]
      },
      "slides": [
        {
          "templateId": "visual-focus",
          "templateSlideKey": "title_identity",
          "templateSlideTitle": "Lesson Title & Identity Slide",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 1,
          "slideType": "identity",
          "slideTitle": "How Plants Make Food: Photosynthesis",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO-01"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Introduction to Lesson Topic"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "To capture student attention and set the context for today's discovery of how plants survive.",
          "studentTakeaway": "I am about to learn the secret process that keeps all life on Earth alive.",
          "layout": "hero-image-left-text-right",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "Subject: Science (Biology)",
            "Grade 7 - Chapter 12",
            "Session 1 of 3",
            "Essential Question: How do plants eat without mouths?"
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "How Plants Make Food",
            "The Process of Photosynthesis",
            "Can a plant survive in the dark?",
            "Let's discover how nature cooks its own food."
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Welcome class! Today we start our journey into the hidden world inside leaves.",
            "Ask students: 'If you were hungry, where would your food come from?' (Answer: Store/Fridge).",
            "Then ask: 'Plants don't have stores or fridges. Where does their food come from?'",
            "Explain that plants are unique because they make their own food using sunlight.",
            "This is the foundation of almost all life on Earth; without this, we would starve.",
            "Transition: To understand how they do it, let's look at what our bodies need to eat."
          ],
          "visualAttribution": {
            "visualPlan": "High-quality close-up photograph of a green leaf with sunlight filtering through the veins against a dark background to highlight chlorophyll.",
            "svgDiagram": null
          },
          "visualPlan": "Hero image: A vibrant, backlit green leaf showing vein structure and light rays hitting it. Text overlay on right side in white box for contrast.",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Visual hook to represent the subject matter",
              "searchQuery": "backlit green leaf sunlight macro photography",
              "sourceSite": "Unsplash Education",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "CC0 / Creative Commons",
              "attributionText": "Image by Science Visuals via Unsplash",
              "altText": "Close-up of a green leaf with sunlight rays penetrating through the veins.",
              "placementHint": "Left 60% of slide, full height."
            }
          ],
          "svgDiagram": {
            "title": "",
            "type": "",
            "instructions": [],
            "svgCode": ""
          },
          "animationHints": [
            "Fade in the title text from bottom.",
            "Animate sunlight rays to pulse gently."
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 2
        },
        {
          "templateId": "visual-focus",
          "templateSlideKey": "learning_outcomes",
          "templateSlideTitle": "Learning Outcomes & Success Criteria",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 2,
          "slideType": "outcomes",
          "slideTitle": "What We Will Learn Today",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO-01",
            "LO-02"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Learning Goals Definition"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "To provide clear, measurable goals so students know exactly what success looks like by the end of class.",
          "studentTakeaway": "I will be able to explain how plants make food and list what they need to do it.",
          "layout": "split-card-list",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "Define photosynthesis as 'making food with light'.",
            "Identify the three essential ingredients: Sunlight, Water, CO2.",
            "Explain why oxygen is released and who needs it.",
            "Understand that plants are the primary producers of our world."
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "By the end of this lesson:",
            "1. Define Photosynthesis",
            "2. Identify Inputs (Sunlight, Water, CO2)",
            "3. Explain Oxygen Release"
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Read these outcomes aloud with the class.",
            "Emphasize that 'Photos' means light and 'Synthesis' means putting together.",
            "Ask: 'Why is it important to know this?' (Answer: Because we breathe oxygen they make, and eat plants or animals that ate them).",
            "Connect LO-3 to the fact that without photosynthesis, there would be no air for us to breathe right now.",
            "Transition: Now let's check what you already know about how things grow."
          ],
          "visualAttribution": {
            "visualPlan": null,
            "svgDiagram": null
          },
          "visualPlan": "Clean list layout with icons next to each outcome. Icon 1: Book/Definition. Icon 2: Sun/Water/Droplet. Icon 3: Lungs/Oxygen molecule.",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Iconography for visual reinforcement of outcomes",
              "searchQuery": "science icons definition sun water oxygen simple vector",
              "sourceSite": "Wikimedia Commons Icons",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "Public Domain / CC-BY-SA",
              "attributionText": "Icons adapted from Wikimedia Commons",
              "altText": "Simple line icons representing definition, sun/water, and oxygen.",
              "placementHint": "Left aligned next to each bullet point."
            }
          ],
          "svgDiagram": {
            "title": "",
            "type": "",
            "instructions": [],
            "svgCode": ""
          },
          "animationHints": [
            "Animate icons appearing one by one as the outcome is read.",
            "Highlight text in green when discussing 'Sunlight' and 'Water'."
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 3
        },
        {
          "templateId": "visual-focus",
          "templateSlideKey": "prerequisite_knowledge",
          "templateSlideTitle": "What We Already Know (Prerequisites)",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 3,
          "slideType": "activation",
          "slideTitle": "Do Plants Eat Like Us?",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO-01"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Prior Knowledge Activation"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "To activate prior knowledge about human nutrition and contrast it with plant biology.",
          "studentTakeaway": "I realize that plants are different from animals because they don't eat food in the same way.",
          "layout": "comparison-table",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "Humans need to find food (apples, bread).",
            "Plants stay rooted in one place.",
            "How can a plant get energy if it cannot move?",
            "Recall: What do we breathe out? Plants take that."
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "Humans:",
            "- Move to find food",
            "- Breathe Oxygen, Exhale CO2",
            "",
            "Plants:",
            "- Stay in one place (Rooted)",
            "- ?"
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Ask: 'How do you get energy when you are hungry?' Students will say eat food.",
            "Ask: 'Can a plant run to the kitchen? No. So how does it survive?'",
            "Prompt students about breathing: 'When we breathe, what gas comes out of our mouths?' (Carbon Dioxide).",
            "Explain that plants actually use this CO2 which is waste for us but food for them.",
            "This sets up the mystery: Plants take in a gas and sunlight to make their own energy.",
            "Transition: Let's look at the big picture of how they do it."
          ],
          "visualAttribution": {
            "visualPlan": null,
            "svgDiagram": null
          },
          "visualPlan": "Split screen comparison. Left side: Child eating an apple (human). Right side: Plant in soil with sun rays (plant).",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Visual contrast between animal and plant behavior",
              "searchQuery": "child eating fruit vs plant growing vector illustration",
              "sourceSite": "Khan Academy Visuals / Wikimedia",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "CC-BY-SA",
              "attributionText": "Illustration adapted for educational comparison",
              "altText": "Comparison of a child eating and a plant growing.",
              "placementHint": "Split screen, 50% width each."
            }
          ],
          "svgDiagram": {
            "title": "",
            "type": "",
            "instructions": [],
            "svgCode": ""
          },
          "animationHints": [
            "Fade in the 'Humans' side first, then animate a question mark on the plant side.",
            "Animate an arrow from human exhalation (CO2) pointing towards the plant."
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 4
        },
        {
          "templateId": "visual-focus",
          "templateSlideKey": "lesson_hook",
          "templateSlideTitle": "The Mystery of the Dark Room",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 4,
          "slideType": "hook",
          "slideTitle": "What Happens in the Dark?",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO-02"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Curiosity Generation / Phenomenon"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "To create a cognitive conflict that drives the need for understanding photosynthesis.",
          "studentTakeaway": "I suspect sunlight is essential because plants die without it, but I don't know why yet.",
          "layout": "scenario-card",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "Imagine two identical potted plants.",
            "Plant A: Placed in a sunny window.",
            "Plant B: Locked in a dark cupboard for 2 weeks.",
            "Prediction: Which one will survive? Why?"
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "The Experiment:",
            "- Plant A: Sunny Window",
            "- Plant B: Dark Cupboard (14 Days)",
            "",
            "Question:",
            "Which plant is still alive?",
            "What was missing for the second one?"
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Tell a story about two friends who got plants. One put theirs on the windowsill, the other in a closet.",
            "Ask students to vote: 'Who thinks Plant A is still green?' (Most will).",
            "Ask: 'What happened to Plant B? It turned yellow and died.'",
            "Push for reasoning: 'Why did it die if we watered both plants equally?'",
            "Guide them to realize that something else besides water was missing. That thing is Sunlight.",
            "This hook establishes the critical role of light energy in plant survival.",
            "Transition: Now let's name this process and see exactly how it works."
          ],
          "visualAttribution": {
            "visualPlan": null,
            "svgDiagram": null
          },
          "visualPlan": "Illustration showing two pots. Left pot has a sun icon above it with green leaves. Right pot is in shadow/darkness with wilting yellow leaves.",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Visual representation of the thought experiment",
              "searchQuery": "plant growing vs dying dark cupboard illustration",
              "sourceSite": "Educational Illustrations / Wikimedia",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "CC-BY-SA",
              "attributionText": "Illustration by Science Education Team",
              "altText": "Comparison of a healthy plant in sun and a dying plant in darkness.",
              "placementHint": "Center stage, large visual."
            }
          ],
          "svgDiagram": {
            "title": "",
            "type": "",
            "instructions": [],
            "svgCode": ""
          },
          "animationHints": [
            "Animate the sun icon to shine brightly on Plant A.",
            "Fade in a 'X' mark over Plant B's leaves."
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 3
        },
        {
          "templateId": "visual-focus",
          "templateSlideKey": "topic_introduction",
          "templateSlideTitle": "Defining Photosynthesis",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 5,
          "slideType": "concept-intro",
          "slideTitle": "What is Photosynthesis?",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO-01"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Definition and Etymology"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "To provide a precise scientific definition and break down the word to aid memory.",
          "studentTakeaway": "Photosynthesis is 'making food using light'.",
          "layout": "definition-box",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "Definition: The process by which green plants make their own food.",
            "Etymology:",
            "- Photo = Light (Greek)",
            "- Synthesis = Putting together",
            "",
            "Key Vocabulary: Chlorophyll, Stomata"
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "Photosynthesis",
            "= Photos + Synthesis",
            "= Making Food with Sunlight",
            "",
            "Requires:",
            "- Green Plants (with Chlorophyll)",
            "- Light Energy"
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Write the word 'PHOTOSYNTHESIS' on the board.",
            "Break it down: 'Photo' means light. 'Synthesis' means putting together or making something new.",
            "So, Photosynthesis literally means 'Making things with Light'.",
            "Explain that this is unique to plants and some bacteria; animals cannot do this.",
            "Introduce the term 'Chlorophyll': The green pigment inside leaves that acts like a solar panel.",
            "Without chlorophyll, there can be no photosynthesis. That's why non-green parts of plants don't make food.",
            "Transition: Now let's look at exactly what ingredients are needed for this cooking process."
          ],
          "visualAttribution": {
            "visualPlan": null,
            "svgDiagram": null
          },
          "visualPlan": "Word cloud or etymology breakdown. 'PHOTO' (Sun icon) + 'SYNTHESIS' (Gear/Assembly icon) = PLANT FOOD.",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Visual aid for word origin",
              "searchQuery": "etymology photo synthesis light making diagram simple vector",
              "sourceSite": "Wikimedia Commons / Educational Graphics",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "Public Domain",
              "attributionText": "Etymology graphic adapted from educational resources",
              "altText": "Visual breakdown of the word Photosynthesis.",
              "placementHint": "Top center or right side."
            }
          ],
          "svgDiagram": {
            "title": "",
            "type": "",
            "instructions": [],
            "svgCode": ""
          },
          "animationHints": [
            "Animate the word parts appearing sequentially.",
            "Highlight 'Light' in yellow and 'Making Food' in green."
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 4
        },
        {
          "templateId": "visual-focus",
          "templateSlideKey": "core_concept_a_visual",
          "templateSlideTitle": "The Inputs: What Plants Need",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 6,
          "slideType": "concept-visual",
          "slideTitle": "Ingredients for Plant Food",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO-02"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Inputs: Sunlight, Water, Carbon Dioxide"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "To visually demonstrate the three essential inputs required for photosynthesis to occur.",
          "studentTakeaway": "Plants need sunlight, water from roots, and carbon dioxide from air.",
          "layout": "process-flow",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "1. Sunlight (Energy Source)",
            "2. Water (H2O) - Absorbed by Roots",
            "3. Carbon Dioxide (CO2) - From Air via Leaves"
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "The Recipe for Food:",
            "- ☀️ Sunlight (Energy)",
            "- 💧 Water (From Soil/Roots)",
            "- 🌬️ CO2 Gas (From Air/Stomata)"
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Explain that photosynthesis is like a recipe. You need specific ingredients.",
            "Ingredient 1: Sunlight. This provides the energy to start the reaction. No light = no cooking.",
            "Ingredient 2: Water. Plants drink this through their roots, just like we do with straws (xylem).",
            "Ingredient 3: Carbon Dioxide. We breathe it out; plants take it in through tiny holes called stomata on leaves.",
            "Emphasize that all three must be present simultaneously for the process to work.",
            "If any one is missing, photosynthesis stops (e.g., drought or cloudy days).",
            "Transition: Now let's see how these ingredients combine inside a leaf."
          ],
          "visualAttribution": {
            "visualPlan": null,
            "svgDiagram": null
          },
          "visualPlan": "Three distinct icons arranged horizontally representing the inputs. Sun icon with rays, Water droplet rising from roots, CO2 molecule entering leaves.",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Iconography for inputs",
              "searchQuery": "sunlight water carbon dioxide plant biology icons vector set",
              "sourceSite": "Wikimedia Commons / OpenClipart",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "CC0 / Public Domain",
              "attributionText": "Icons adapted from educational resources",
              "altText": "Simple icons representing Sun, Water, and CO2.",
              "placementHint": "Top row of slide."
            }
          ],
          "svgDiagram": {
            "title": "",
            "type": "",
            "instructions": [],
            "svgCode": ""
          },
          "animationHints": [
            "Animate arrows flowing from each icon towards a central leaf image.",
            "Fade in the icons one by one as they are mentioned."
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 5
        },
        {
          "templateId": "visual-focus",
          "templateSlideKey": "core_concept_b_visual",
          "templateSlideTitle": "The Process Inside a Leaf",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 7,
          "slideType": "concept-visual",
          "slideTitle": "How the Magic Happens",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO-02"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Mechanism of Photosynthesis in Leaves"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "To provide a detailed visual model showing how inputs are converted into outputs within the leaf structure.",
          "studentTakeaway": "Chlorophyll captures light, and water + CO2 combine to make sugar (food) and oxygen.",
          "layout": "diagram-heavy",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "- Chloroplasts contain Chlorophyll",
            "- Light energy splits Water molecules",
            "- CO2 combines with Hydrogen to form Glucose",
            "- Oxygen is released as a byproduct"
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "Inside the Leaf:",
            "1. Sunlight hits Chloroplasts (Green)",
            "2. Water + CO2 enter",
            "3. Energy converts them to Glucose (Food) & Oxygen",
            "",
            "Result: Food for Plant, Air for Us"
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Point to the diagram on Slide 7.",
            "Explain that inside every green cell are tiny factories called chloroplasts containing chlorophyll (the green stuff).",
            "Describe the reaction: Sunlight hits the chlorophyll. This energy breaks apart water molecules absorbed by roots.",
            "The hydrogen from water combines with Carbon Dioxide to create Glucose (a type of sugar/food for the plant).",
            "Crucially, Oxygen is left over and released into the air through stomata.",
            "This explains why we need plants: they turn our waste gas (CO2) into fresh oxygen.",
            "Transition: Let's see this process in action with a real-world example."
          ],
          "visualAttribution": {
            "visualPlan": null,
            "svgDiagram": null
          },
          "visualPlan": "Detailed cross-section diagram of a leaf showing roots taking up water, stomata letting CO2 in, sunlight hitting chloroplasts inside cells, and oxygen exiting.",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Core scientific illustration",
              "searchQuery": "photosynthesis process leaf cross section diagram labeled vector",
              "sourceSite": "Khan Academy / Wikimedia Commons",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "CC-BY-SA",
              "attributionText": "Diagram adapted from Science Education Resources",
              "altText": "Labeled diagram of photosynthesis process in a leaf.",
              "placementHint": "Full slide background or large central element."
            }
          ],
          "svgDiagram": {
            "title": "",
            "type": "",
            "instructions": [],
            "svgCode": ""
          },
          "animationHints": [
            "Animate the flow of water from roots up to leaves.",
            "Animate CO2 entering stomata and Oxygen exiting.",
            "Flash yellow light on chloroplasts when explaining energy capture."
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 6
        },
        {
          "templateId": "visual-focus",
          "templateSlideKey": "worked_example",
          "templateSlideTitle": "Real-World Application: The Apple Tree",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 8,
          "slideType": "example",
          "slideTitle": "Case Study: An Apple Tree",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO-02"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Application of Photosynthesis to Fruit Growth"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "To connect the abstract biological process to a tangible, familiar object (an apple).",
          "studentTakeaway": "The sugar in an apple was made by photosynthesis inside the tree's leaves.",
          "layout": "case-study-card",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "- Tree absorbs water from soil roots.",
            "- Leaves take CO2 from air.",
            "- Sunlight powers the reaction.",
            "- Result: Sugar stored in fruit (Apple).",
            "- Oxygen released for us to breathe."
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "The Apple Tree Example:",
            "Inputs: Water + CO2 + Sunlight",
            "-> Process inside Leaves",
            "-> Output: Glucose (Sugar) stored in Fruit!",
            "",
            "You are eating the product of photosynthesis!"
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Show a picture of an apple tree.",
            "Ask: 'Where does this sweet taste come from?'",
            "Explain that the sugar inside the apple was not put there by farmers; it was made in the leaves via photosynthesis and transported down to the fruit.",
            "This is why apples are sweet. The plant stored its food energy as sugar for later use (or for us!).",
            "Reiterate: When you eat an apple, you are eating captured sunlight!",
            "Transition: Now it's your turn to practice identifying these parts."
          ],
          "visualAttribution": {
            "visualPlan": null,
            "svgDiagram": null
          },
          "visualPlan": "Image of a red apple with an arrow pointing back to the tree leaves and sun.",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Visual connection between fruit and process",
              "searchQuery": "apple on tree sunlight diagram educational illustration",
              "sourceSite": "Wikimedia Commons / Educational Graphics",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "CC-BY-SA",
              "attributionText": "Illustration by Science Education Team",
              "altText": "Apple hanging from a tree with sun rays.",
              "placementHint": "Center of slide."
            }
          ],
          "svgDiagram": {
            "title": "",
            "type": "",
            "instructions": [],
            "svgCode": ""
          },
          "animationHints": [
            "Animate an arrow flowing from the leaves to the apple.",
            "Highlight 'Sugar' text in yellow."
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 4
        },
        {
          "templateId": "visual-focus",
          "templateSlideKey": "guided_practice",
          "templateSlideTitle": "Class Activity: The Plant Detective",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 9,
          "slideType": "activity",
          "slideTitle": "Activity: Identify the Missing Ingredient",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO-02"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Application and Problem Solving"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "To engage students in active learning by diagnosing why a plant is not growing.",
          "studentTakeaway": "I can identify which input (light, water, or CO2) is missing based on symptoms.",
          "layout": "interactive-scenario",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "- Scenario: A houseplant looks yellow and weak.",
            "- Clue 1: It was in a dark closet for weeks.",
            "- Question: What ingredient did it miss?",
            "- Action: Discuss with your partner."
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "Detective Work:",
            "",
            "Plant Status: Yellow leaves, weak stem",
            "Location: Dark Cupboard (No Sun)",
            "Watered Daily? Yes.",
            "",
            "Question: What is missing?",
            "- A) Water",
            "- B) Carbon Dioxide",
            "- C) Sunlight"
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Divide class into pairs for 2 minutes of discussion (Think-Pair-Share).",
            "Present the scenario: 'Here is a plant that was watered but put in a dark closet.'",
            "Ask students to discuss which ingredient caused the problem.",
            "After time, ask groups to vote. Most should choose Sunlight.",
            "Confirm answer C and explain why (No energy source = no photosynthesis).",
            "Extension: 'What if it was in sunlight but we forgot water?' (Answer: Water is also essential).",
            "Transition: Let's check what you've learned with a quick quiz."
          ],
          "visualAttribution": {
            "visualPlan": null,
            "svgDiagram": null
          },
          "visualPlan": "Illustration of a wilting yellow plant next to a dark closet icon.",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Visual for the problem scenario",
              "searchQuery": "wilting houseplant illustration vector simple",
